name: Pošćenska jezera (Pošćensko jezero) — warm, empty Durmitor swim lakes
description: A cluster of glacial lakes on the Jezerska Površ plateau ~7.5km south of Žabljak, right on the macadam Žabljak→Trsa road towards Sedlo. Far shallower (max 3.6m) than Crno/Vražje jezero, so the water actually warms up — a genuine summer SWIM lake, ringed by pasture and forest, with almost none of Black Lake's crowds. A negative-space find: every other Durmitor lake the group has is either cold-and-famous or a hard hike; this is the easy, quiet swim.
communityNotes: Local/Žabljak tourism and Photo Montenegro consistently file these as 'gorske oči' that geologists, travel writers and hikers come for; durmitor.rs notes the big lake is '210x150x3.5m, summer is perfect for swimming and fishing, in winter it freezes.' No English Reddit chatter = exactly why it's still empty. Drive-by access (the macadam to Trsa is fine for a normal car in dry June) means you can stop for 30 min on the way to/from the Sedlo Pass without a dedicated hike.
bestTime: Midday June for warmest water; pairs with the Sedlo Pass drive

name: Zabojsko jezero — the forest 'mountain eye' of Sinjajevina
description: Montenegro's third-deepest lake (19m), a near-perfect dark oval ringed by dense spruce-and-beech forest on the NE edge of the Sinjajevina massif. Sinjajevina is the biggest mountain in the country yet almost untouristed — while Durmitor is a 'tourist mecca, Sinjajevina remained aside, unknown.' A drivable macadam reaches a lakeside meadow with benches, tables and a spring; ideal wild-camp or picnic + cold swim, with deer and few people.
communityNotes: Total Montenegro News dubs it 'the mountain eye of Sinjajevina'; Montenegro Adventure runs hikes here precisely because it's off-radar. Tourism sources: 'the lake is ideal for camping; on the meadow near the lake, wooden benches and tables are set, with a cafe nearby... a drinking water source.' Sits between Žabljak and Mojkovac — a natural detour if the route swings east toward Biogradska/Komovi. Forest setting means shade + total quiet rather than alpine drama.
cost: Free (wild camp); small cafe on site

name: Pešića jezero + Eko Katun Vranjak (Bjelasica)
description: Bjelasica's second-largest glacial lake at ~1,830m under Crna Glava/Zekova Glava — a green mirror in the high pastures, reached on foot from Eko Katun Vranjak (1,800m), a real working shepherd settlement turned rustic homestay. You can drive a normal car to the Kolašin 1600 ski centre and on to Vranjak, sleep/eat in the katuns, then hike to the lake. The deep-backcountry alternative to the crowded Biogradsko jezero lake loop the group already has.
communityNotes: TripAdvisor Montenegro forum (a local mountaineer): a regular car safely reaches the ski centre and Eko Katun Vranjak; the final macadam to Zekova Glava 'could be easily impassable in spring because of snow/ice' but is fine once snow-free — 'almost entirely snow free by late May.' Crucial timing: 'Eko katun Vranjak does not open until the beginning of summer' → mid/late June is the first window. Jonty (2024 trek report) stayed two nights at the tourist-developed katun huts ('toilet and shower block') in near-total 'solitude,' meeting only farmers. At Vranjak you can 'try homemade food, ride horses... milk cows' — authentic katun life.
timeNeeded: Half/full day hike from Vranjak; overnight katun option
bestTime: Katun opens early summer — late June onward; clear morning

name: Bukumirsko jezero — dragon-legend lake of Kučka krajina
description: A glacial lake at 1,440m cupped by the Kučka krajina peaks east of Podgorica — '~45km, steep narrow winding road' but asphalt nearly to the lake (then a 15-min walk). Snow-streaked summits, grazing horses and eagles overhead, ~20°C air when Podgorica bakes at 35°C; warm enough to swim by midsummer and ringed by camp-able meadows. Carries a proper dragon legend and an endemic newt (žmigavac). A different region entirely from the group's Grebaje/Grlo Sokolovo cluster.
communityNotes: First-person blog (Living in Montenegro): 'The stunning panorama of the Lake with the peaks of the Kučka Krajina dominating over it is truly one of the most beautiful mountain landscapes of Montenegro'; they found only 'a group of young people' there. Drive: turn left ~20km past Ubli toward Bukumirsko jezero; asphalt ends, park, 15-min walk. Discover-Montenegro: water 'clear and transparent, visibility up to 4m,' an 'ad-hoc camping place,' swimming popular in summer. Legend: villagers heated stones in fire and threw them in to drive out a lake dragon.
timeNeeded: Half day incl. drive from Podgorica (~1h15 each way)
bestTime: Midsummer for swim; weekday for solitude

name: Rikavačko jezero — biggest Kučka krajina lake, on the Albanian rim
description: The largest glacial lake of the Kučka krajina (525x230m, ~14m deep) at 1,313m, sitting in raw highland a few hundred metres from the Albanian border between the Širokar plateau and Mt Vila. Far wilder and emptier than Bukumirsko — a backcountry add-on for the group's deep-east day, with brisk swimming and katun-dotted meadows. Home to the endemic Montenegrin newt.
communityNotes: Discover-Montenegro: 'the largest lake of Kučka krajina... close to Rikavac Katun and just a few hundred metres from the Albanian border'; described as a base for climbs to Pasjak. Lustica Bay/local features call it a 'mythical mountain escape.' Swimming 'brisk but refreshing in the peak of summer.' Genuinely remote — the road in is rough highland macadam; best as a paired wild day with Bukumirsko or Grlo Sokolovo. Border zone: carry passports, don't stray across.
timeNeeded: Half/full day with the rough drive
bestTime: Peak summer (snow lingers late); clear weather only

name: Karuč + Karučko Oko — the secret stone village & spring-eye of Skadar
description: A near-abandoned stone fishing hamlet on a quiet arm of Lake Skadar, reached by a hairy single-track road (or 20-min boat taxi from Rijeka Crnojevića). Its prize is Karučko Oko — a deep (~20m) sublacustrine spring 'eye' that stays cool and crystal-clear all summer, a perfect swim/SUP launch into lily-pad 'Amazon' channels — plus a viewpoint over the rooftops and meanders, and the ruined winter house of Petar I Petrović Njegoš. A true mimo-turista alternative to crowded Virpazar.
communityNotes: Adriatic Ways: 'If Rijeka Crnojevića is the historic capital, Karuč is the lake's best-kept secret, a crumbling stone village where time has stood still for centuries'; 'total absence of parking hustlers,' 'a landscape of profound silence.' Official Montenegro.travel: 'peace, authenticity and untouched nature,' homemade smoked fish, locals offer boat tours through the channels. Karučko Oko = 'the village's natural swimming hole, framed by lily pads and ancient stone walls... deep and cool.' Access: 'single-track road with sharp bends and steep drops' off the Podgorica–Cetinje road; cash only, no ATMs.
cost: Free (boat taxi from Rijeka Crnojevića extra)
bestTime: Morning for mirror water; avoid midday-only crowds (none early)

name: Murići beach — Skadar's biggest, oak-shaded, bath-warm beach
description: The largest beach on the Montenegrin side of Lake Skadar (600m), backed by ancient oak and chestnut forest under the Rumija massif, reached by a stunning single-lane serpentine south from Virpazar. Freshwater hits a 'bath-like' ~30°C in summer — a warm-water swim when the early-June Adriatic is still cold — and you can hire a local boatman to the 14th-c. Beška island monastery. Donkeys and goats laze on the pebbles; rarely crowded.
communityNotes: Adriatic Ways (local guide): 'a world where time slows down — an untouched beach where you might find the occasional donkey or goat lying peacefully on the pebbles beside you'; '~30°C bath-like' water, 'rarely crowded,' parking €2-3, no entry fee, boat to Beška ~€20. HONEST CAVEATS: the serpentine is 'narrow, winding, largely single-lane, not for beginner drivers'; and off-season the currents wash Albanian plastic ashore — June is the cusp, so it can be clean or have some litter. Best early before the few day-trippers arrive.
bestTime: Mid-June onward for warm water; arrive early; check litter locally

name: Komarnica canyon (Dragišnica i Komarnica NP) — wild packraft/hike, Šavnik
description: The open river canyon downstream of the famous Nevidio slot — a Nature Park since 2023, untouched, with green pools, class II-III rapids and trout, run by packraft (or hiked along) from Brezna village to a Piva-lake takeout. Unlike Nevidio's wetsuit gauntlet, this is a beginner-friendly wilderness float you can do without canyoning skills. Threatened by a planned 150m dam, so it's a 'go now' gem squarely on the Plužine→Šavnik corridor.
communityNotes: Balkan Expeditions (Aug 2024 report): put-in at Brezna, carry kit down to the river; 'we are completely alone in the canyon, it is a great privilege to be in a place like this!'; class II-III, 'not very fast... ideal for beginners,' water ~10°C, motorboat retrieval at Piva lake, ~3-4h. Only operator: local guide Gigo Bulajić. 'Even when the heat rages in the cities, it's ~20° lower in the canyon.' Park Šavnik markets canyoning/hiking/cycling here; the canyon is 'completely untouched by human influence,' settlements (Brezna, Duži) sit high above. Distinct from our existing Nevidio entry.
cost: Guided packraft tour (operator-priced); free to hike sections
timeNeeded: 3-4h packraft; can be a full day
bestTime: Summer water levels; cold water — bring layers

name: Vodopad Crnjak — hidden 20m waterfall above Šavnik
description: A 20m waterfall at the source of the Bijela river near Šavnik, with the visible ruins of an 800-year-old watermill at its foot — a true skriveni biser in the Nikšić–Šavnik dead zone of the group's map. Water never exceeds 7-8°C; locals still come to brave a swim. A short scenic stop if routing inland between Nikšić/Plužine and Žabljak.
communityNotes: Mondo.me local feature calls it 'a hidden gem of Šavnik': 20m drop, water '7-8°C,' an '800-year-old mill once powered by the rushing water,' and 'locals report people do come to swim.' Very little tourist info exists — part of the appeal. No formal trail signage found; treat the final approach as a short rough walk and ask in Šavnik for the Bijela-source turnoff. Pairs naturally with the Komarnica/Nevidio area.
bestTime: Late spring/early summer for strongest flow

name: Slansko jezero — island-studded quiet lake by Nikšić
description: A large (9km²) reservoir west of Nikšić with a deeply indented, island-and-peninsula shoreline and clean clear water — far emptier than the group's existing Krupac lake stop, with a lakeside viewpoint and easy walks. A low-key swim/paddle/sunset break on the Nikšić belt if the route passes through (e.g. Ostrog ↔ Plužine).
communityNotes: Local features call it 'a hidden gem... serene beauty'; 'the coastline is rugged, with a lot of islands and peninsulas, and the waters are incredibly clean and clear.' Komoot lists quiet shoreline walks; an evendo 'Slano jezero vidikovac' viewpoint gives the panorama. It's a working hydro reservoir (created 1950) so water level can drop late summer, but June is reliable. Use as a calm alternative/companion to Krupac rather than a headline stop.
bestTime: June (higher water); evening for the viewpoint

name: Gornjepoljski vir — the Balkans' biggest estavelle (TIMING CAVEAT)
description: The largest estavelle in the Balkans (declared a natural monument 2014) ~12km from Nikšić — a giant karst pool that acts as a powerful spring in the wet half-year and reverses into a swallow-hole that sucks the river underground in summer. A genuinely weird geological curiosity almost no tourists visit. HONEST FLAG: it's a SPRING only Oct–May; by mid-late June it's transitioning to a dry sink, so the group would likely see the bare ponor/whirlpool basin, not gushing water — include only if karst geology fascinates them.
communityNotes: ShowCaves/Mondo: 'an estavelle... a spring between October and May and a swallow hole during the summer'; the spectacular 'pucanje vira' (bursting) outburst is an AUTUMN event. Share Montenegro: 'biggest estavelle on the Balkans... still remains a secret for tourists.' So the wow-factor is seasonal and mostly NOT in June. Reachable easily by car. Worth a 20-min curiosity stop only if already near Nikšić; manage expectations on water.
bestTime: Oct-May for active spring; June = likely dry sink

name: Klinci — abandoned olive-grove ghost hamlet on Luštica
description: A centuries-old stone village in the olive groves of the inner Luštica peninsula, almost fully abandoned and only now slowly reviving as rural tourism — narrow lanes, ruined and half-restored houses, laurel woods and silence a few km from Rose. A short atmospheric wander 'away from the beach for a few hours,' and the natural land-base for walking down to the boat-only Dobreč cove.
communityNotes: Luštica guides: 'a hidden gem, recently completely abandoned, where rural tourism is slowly but surely developing,' surrounded by 'olive trees, pomegranate trees and woods of laurel.' Lustica Bay feature: 'very high' seclusion, 'little traffic,' 'ideal for travellers who want to step away from the beach.' Best paired with Arza/Mirište and Dobreč for a half-day inland-Luštica loop the group's coast plan currently skips entirely. Narrow local roads — drive slowly.
bestTime: Late afternoon light; cooler for walking

name: Arza fortress + Mirište cove (Austro-Hungarian, Luštica tip)
description: A late-19th-c. Austro-Hungarian coastal fort on Cape Mirišta at the open-sea tip of Luštica, part of the 'Primorska tvrđava Boka' system with Mamula and Prevlaka. Reached by a 500m walk from the sheltered Mirište pebble cove (or by boat/kayak), with clear snorkel water below and a tiny church on an offshore islet. The fort building itself is private/closed, but you can walk right around it for the views and the abandoned-military atmosphere.
communityNotes: Multiple sources: built 1850s, 'one of Montenegro's most striking coastal fortifications,' on Cape Mirišta at ~42°23'24"N 18°33'57"E. 'Reached by walking mountain paths, or by driving to Mirište beach and walking 500m.' HONEST FLAG: 'nowadays the fortress is private property and closed to visitors — however you are free to walk around it.' So treat it as an exterior/viewpoint + swim, not an interior tour. Mirište is a calm family cove; Arza adds the wild, historic edge. Snorkelling 'clear blue waters' all around.
bestTime: Morning before Žanjic boats arrive nearby

name: Dobreč — boat/walk-only pine-and-cliff cove (Luštica)
description: A narrow pebble cove enclosed by pine forest and limestone cliffs with a small sea cave, on the outer Luštica coast — no road right to it, so it's reached by water taxi/boat or a walk down from Klinci, keeping it genuinely secluded. Calm, clear water that's excellent for swimming, snorkelling and SUP, with a beach bar in season.
communityNotes: Lustica Bay: 'keeps a strong sense of seclusion that is increasingly rare,' 'one of the best Luštica beaches for swimming, paddleboarding'; 'calm, clear water.' Reached 'by boat or water taxi only' (or the inland walk from Klinci). Because access is deliberately awkward it stays quiet even in season — the antidote to packed Žanjic. Pair with Klinci (park there, walk down) for a half-day. Bring water/food; limited facilities.

name: Yugoslav submarine tunnels (Rose / Luštica) — swim inside
description: Three Cold-War-era submarine pens carved into the Luštica headland near Rose by the Yugoslav Navy, their mouths camouflaged with fake rock to hide from satellites. Each is ~100m long over 10m-deep water and is a dead-end you can SWIM right into — an eerie, dripping, torch-lit cavern with side walking galleries and abandoned naval relics nearby. Reachable by boat/kayak (Herceg Novi or Žanjic) or, with effort, on foot from Rose.
communityNotes: ShowCaves: tunnels at ~42.4277,18.5900; 'smaller ships can enter... it is possible to swim inside'; walking pathways run along the sides; 'also possible to visit from land starting at Rose, though the entrances are not easy to find'; 'bring a torch.' Strel Swimming runs guided swims through them; speedboat operators bill it 'Montenegro's longest kept secret.' Adventurous note: people 'dive off the top of the cave into the sea.' Best as a kayak/boat add-on from the Žanjic–Rose corner; combine with Blue Cave/Mamula. Watch boat traffic at the entrances.
cost: Free if self-paddled; boat tours operator-priced
bestTime: Calm sea; midday light helps inside

name: Ljutica — world's shortest & most powerful river (Tara, Đurđevića)
description: A 130m-long left tributary that bursts white out of the canyon rock 35m above its confluence with the Tara, ~1.5km downstream of the Đurđevića Tara bridge (pin approximate — confluence on the Tara's left bank below the bridge) — claimed as Europe's shortest and most powerful river ('Tara's pearl breast'). A tiny, fierce, ice-cold cascade most people only glimpse from a raft. HONEST FLAG: it's awkward to reach on foot and most easily seen on the lower rafting section the group already plans — a bonus, not a standalone detour.
communityNotes: Photo/Discover Montenegro: 'the shortest and most powerful river in the Old World,' length 130m, snowmelt flow can exceed 100 m³/s, water 12-16°C, nicknamed 'Tara's breast pearl.' Rafters report the run 'begins with the waterfalls of Ljutica' before passing under the bridge. Land access is steep/unmarked, so realistically it's a rafting-trip highlight rather than a self-drive stop — flagged for honesty given the group already has Tara rafting.
timeNeeded: Bonus stop on a raft trip
